caveats: a: A single addressability estimate masks very different airbag, thermal, exhaust, wheel, and fluid-component processes.

a: The range measures current technical exposure, not realized labor elimination.

rho: Weak MES, sensor, and defect-label data are common adoption bottlenecks.

e: Establishment counts do not map cleanly to firms or acquisition platforms.

s5: Combustion-specific and powertrain-neutral suppliers likely have different buyer pools.

q: Input-cost pass-throughs can obscure underlying productivity sharing.

d5: A single program award or loss can dominate an LMM supplier's result.
d5: Tariffs and localization may move production among plants without changing end demand.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Suppliers earn per-part revenue under OEM or Tier customer platform awards and production releases, often with separate or negotiated tooling, engineering, testing, and launch economics. Awards can last a vehicle program but quantities are not guaranteed; customer quality, traceability, warranty, delivery, and annual productivity expectations govern the relationship.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to independent recurring-production OEM and Tier suppliers because this residual code spans dissimilar products, aftermarket and rebuilding activities, captive and global operations, and suppliers whose AI exposure and demand outlook—especially between combustion-specific and powertrain-neutral parts—are not economically comparable.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive OEM operations, global integrated megasuppliers, aftermarket brands and distributors, remanufacturers, prototype-only shops, businesses primarily classified in another named motor-vehicle-parts category, and firms without repeat production-program revenue.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent OEM and Tier automotive suppliers making recurring-program components captured by the residual 336390 category, including thermal and HVAC systems, fluid and air-management parts, filters, airbags and restraint components, wheels, frames, bumpers, radiators, fuel tanks, exhaust-system parts, and related assemblies.

a.bridge: A3's cross-manufacturing evidence on AI vision, process validation, inventory, quality, and welding is mapped to the varied workflows of eligible 336390 suppliers, using Autoliv's safety products and ABC Technologies' fluid, HVAC, flexible-product, tooling, testing, and assembly scope to bound the task mix. The estimate includes inspection, test analytics, planning, maintenance, quoting, engineering support, traceability, and quality documentation, while excluding already-automated machinery and irreducible forming, molding, sewing, assembly, handling, and test execution. Large-company and vendor cases likely overstate LMM data readiness; the category's product variety widens both tails.
caveats: A single addressability estimate masks very different airbag, thermal, exhaust, wheel, and fluid-component processes.
Published AI cases skew toward larger and better-instrumented plants.
The range measures current technical exposure, not realized labor elimination.
a.change_evidence: A task-and-wage census across representative eligible US suppliers, segmented by product family and current automation, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: AI can assist vision inspection, anomaly detection, production and material planning, predictive maintenance, test-data interpretation, quotation, root-cause analysis, warranty review, and quality documentation. Safety, thermal, fluid, exhaust, and structural components still require substantial physical conversion and validated testing, and the exact mix differs sharply across the residual code.

d5.bridge: Strattec's actual North American filing evidence of five-to-seven-year platform lives, production below pre-pandemic levels, and a near-term decline followed by recovery to a flat multiyear market is mapped to the eligible 336390 population. The base is lifted slightly above units because safety and thermal content can rise and EVs require dedicated battery thermal management, as Hanon and HDT describe. The range incorporates platform wins and losses and the opposing trajectories of powertrain-neutral safety/thermal systems versus combustion-specific exhaust, fuel, and filter parts. Company-specific content growth may bias the base upward for the whole residual category.
caveats: The residual code combines product families with opposite powertrain transitions.
A single program award or loss can dominate an LMM supplier's result.
Tariffs and localization may move production among plants without changing end demand.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year, program-weighted demand model by 336390 product family, powertrain, content per vehicle, sourcing region, and scheduled platform launches and sunsets would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Broad North American vehicle production appears mature, but mandatory safety content and increasingly complex thermal management support value per vehicle across powertrains. Conversely, fuel tanks, exhaust-system parts, and some engine-related filters face secular pressure as EV penetration rises. The mixed product basket supports modest aggregate growth with unusually wide dispersion.

caveats: The residual nature of the code creates unusually high classification heterogeneity.
Establishment counts do not map cleanly to firms or acquisition platforms.
A supplier may mix eligible OEM production with aftermarket, rebuilding, or non-automotive work.
e.change_evidence: A plant-level census linked to ownership, size, OEM-versus-aftermarket revenue, product family, repeat-program status, and EBITDA would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: Census materials confirm that 336390 is a residual category covering engine filters, air-conditioning systems, airbag assemblies, wheels, frames, bumpers, radiators, fuel tanks, exhaust parts, and other products. A meaningful independent OEM/Tier supplier population exists, but the full code also includes large global systems companies, captive facilities, aftermarket and rebuilding operations, mixed-market manufacturers, and product families with incompatible economics.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 336390 ($10,154,708,586) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 336390 ($68,828,418,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $483,020 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336390); class <5: 322 firms, avg revenue/firm $603,388 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $54,546 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 134 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,633,784 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $147,694 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 141 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,721,894 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $336,459 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 208 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,889,644 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,165,224 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 145 firms, avg revenue/firm $100,321,055 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,069,023 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 151 firms, avg revenue/firm $335,515,325 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,330,585 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 353

caveats: The low case is concentrated in combustion-specific suppliers rather than evenly spread across the category.
This primitive excludes ordinary unit-cycle demand already represented in d5.
New regulation can add safety or emissions content even as other parts disappear.
o.change_evidence: Platform roadmaps quantifying combustion-specific part run-off, EV thermal and safety content, component consolidation, insourcing, and alternative-material adoption would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Physical safety, thermal, structural, and fluid-management parts cannot be digitized away, but electrification directly displaces some fuel tanks, exhaust-system parts, and engine-filter content included in 336390. Additional outside risks include OEM or Tier-1 insourcing, module consolidation, alternative materials, and designs that integrate multiple parts. Five-year displacement is material but limited because fleet transition is gradual and EVs add thermal and electrical-safety needs.

q.bridge: Lear's disclosed automotive component purchase orders, annual productivity price reductions, ongoing price adjustments, and supplier price-reduction programs are used as a proxy for contracting across the eligible 336390 OEM/Tier supplier population. Smaller suppliers generally have less bargaining power and greater customer concentration, so the proxy may overstate retention; patented safety content, scarce qualifications, proprietary materials, or hidden quality and uptime gains can support the high case.
caveats: Lear is much larger and more diversified than the target lens.
Retention varies by customer, product criticality, patent position, and capacity scarcity.
Input-cost pass-throughs can obscure underlying productivity sharing.
q.change_evidence: A representative set of eligible supplier contracts and negotiation histories showing annual price-downs, indexation, tooling recovery, open-book costing, warranty allocation, and treatment of automation savings would materially change this value.
q.rationale: Vehicle-program awards provide temporary economic protection, but automotive customers regularly demand productivity givebacks and re-source future platforms. Visible labor and cycle-time savings are likely to be shared, while avoided warranty, scrap, downtime, and launch costs can be retained more effectively if they are not embedded in open-book costing.

rho.bridge: A3's deployed industrial-AI cases are translated into five-year realization for smaller automotive component plants and discounted for customer-specific validation, safety-critical failure modes, fragmented legacy systems, cybersecurity, integration cost, model monitoring, and scarce technical staff. Autoliv's emphasis on safety-critical components and testing makes the safety end of the category slower than generic manufacturing, while modular planning and documentation tools can move faster. Vendor case selection likely biases the base upward.
caveats: Realization varies substantially by product family and installed equipment.
Safety-critical decisions may retain human review even after successful AI deployment.
Weak MES, sensor, and defect-label data are common adoption bottlenecks.
rho.change_evidence: Comparable LMM deployments reporting integration cost, validation time, false-positive rates, labor hours saved, scrap, uptime, and scale-up across programs would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Inspection, maintenance, scheduling, document, and analytics tools can be introduced without redesigning the product, but changes affecting process control or release decisions must satisfy demanding automotive quality and traceability requirements. Benefits will often appear in scrap, uptime, throughput, and avoided quality escapes rather than direct payroll reduction.

s5.bridge: Hankook's completed purchase of control of global thermal-management supplier Hanon and HDT's completed acquisition of selected Veritas fluid, HVAC, and thermal-management operations are observed transfers in product families represented in 336390, mapped to the probability of an eligible US LMM supplier changing control within five years. Both transactions are much larger, cross-border, and more integrated than the target lens, and the Veritas transaction involved selected operations, so the base is heavily discounted. Strategic consolidation evidence can overstate availability of smaller family-held firms, while private LMM deals are under-disclosed.
caveats: Both observed precedents are substantially above the LMM target scale.
Veritas was a selected-business acquisition rather than a simple whole-company sale.
Combustion-specific and powertrain-neutral suppliers likely have different buyer pools.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year denominator of independent US 336390 suppliers matched to all control transfers, recapitalizations, closures, and asset sales by product family would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Thermal, fluid-management, safety, and other specialized automotive capabilities can attract strategic and financial buyers, particularly where they add powertrain-neutral or EV content. Availability is constrained by family ownership, program and customer concentration, warranty exposure, capital needs, product obsolescence, and the diligence complexity of a residual multi-product category.

principal_driver: Required safety and increasingly important thermal-management content provides durable recurring vehicle-program demand plus addressable quality and plant-support workflows.
principal_weakness: The residual code's extreme product heterogeneity makes a single industry estimate fragile, and combustion-specific components face secular displacement even as customer price-downs absorb much of the AI upside.
